content: This is a great article on low-level system design. I really liked the explanation on rate limiting algorithms!

replies.content: Agreed! Token bucket vs leaky bucket was explained very clearly. Which one do you prefer for a highly bursty traffic scenario?
For bursty traffic, token bucket is usually preferred because it allows bursty requests up to the capacity of the bucket, whereas leaky bucket smooths out the traffic at a constant rate.
Exactly! But keep in mind that leaky bucket guarantees a smooth output flow, which is crucial for some downstream systems that can't handle sudden spikes.
Depends on the system requirements. If you want to prevent resource exhaustion completely, Leaky Bucket might be safer.
I think the diagram on sliding window logs was a bit confusing. Could anyone clarify how the cleanup of old logs happens?

Every time a new request comes in, we remove all timestamps from the sorted set (like Redis ZREMRANGEBYSCORE) that are older than (current_time - window_size). Then we count the remaining elements in the set.
That makes perfect sense now, thank you! So it does require a database write or update for every single check.
Yes, which is why it can be memory-intensive and expensive if you have a lot of requests. That's why sliding window counter is a nice middle ground.

content: Can we also implement nested comments in React recursively? What is the maximum recursion depth we should be worried about?

replies.content: Yes, recursion in React is as simple as a component calling itself: `<Comment comment={reply} />` inside a map loop. React can easily handle depth up to hundreds of levels, but UI-wise, you might run out of indentation space after 5-7 levels.
Absolutely. Usually websites like Reddit stop indenting or collapse after a certain depth and offer a 'load more/view thread' link to open a new view.
Good point, Reddit style thread handling is neat. Do we have a standard library or package for this in React?
Usually no package is required, it's just recursion. But packages like `react-nested-comments` exist if you want pre-built UI.
Nice! Doing it from scratch is better for LLD interviews anyway.
Don't forget performance! If the list is huge, you might want to look into virtualization or only rendering the visible subtree.
Virtualization packages like `react-window` or `react-virtualized` work well, but integrating them with nested/collapsible tree structures can be tricky.
Indeed. You typically flatten the tree first into a list of items with indentation depths metadata before virtualization.

content: Great UI guidelines! I love the glassmorphic design system proposed. Will definitely try to implement it in my project.

replies.content: Glassmorphism looks great, but make sure you test readability, especially under different screen brightnesses or contrast settings.
Absolutely! Adding a solid background fallback or checking contrast ratios is crucial for accessibility (WCAG).
Are you using Tailwind or custom CSS for the glassmorphism effect?
Usually it's `backdrop-filter: blur(10px); background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2);` in vanilla CSS. Both work perfectly.

content: What's the best strategy to handle state management for nested comments in a vanilla JS implementation?

replies.content: For Vanilla JS, using a normalized state object (flat map/dictionary) where comments are indexed by their ID is extremely helpful. That way, updates, upvotes, or deletes are O(1) operations.
Exactly. Trying to traverse a deep recursive tree directly in JS for an update is O(N) and error-prone.
Also makes it easy to serialize/deserialize and save to localStorage!
You can also use custom events to pass data updates back to the root component.
Yes! Event delegation on the root comments container is key for memory efficiency.

content: How do we handle timestamps dynamically? E.g., showing '2 minutes ago' instead of the raw ISO string.

replies.content: You can write a simple utility helper function that calculates the difference between `Date.now()` and the comment's timestamp.
Be sure to set up a setInterval/timer if you want those relative times to update in real-time without manual page refreshes.
Or use lightweight libraries like `date-fns` or `dayjs` to avoid handling tricky timezone edge cases manually.

content: For the upvote/downvote feature, should the vote state be managed locally in the component or globally in the comments list?

replies.content: Globally is better because voting can affect sorting, and other components might need to know the updated vote count.
Agreed. It also ensures data consistency if you display the same comment elsewhere on the page.

content: Are there any security concerns like XSS when rendering user comments?

replies.content: Yes! Never use `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` in React or direct `innerHTML` in vanilla JS without sanitizing the input first.
`DOMPurify` is a great library for sanitizing HTML inputs before rendering them.
